key_insight: Theophylline monohydrate dehydrates through a two-step, surface-controlled crystallographic transformation that preserves local orientation at a common plane while forming anhydrous form II.
engineering_relevance: This shows how hydrate stability and phase-change behavior can be predicted and managed by tracking surface mass loss, morphology, and lattice orientation in beam-sensitive molecular crystals.
method: In situ low-dose scanning electron diffraction (SED)
Simultaneous mapping of morphology, phase, and crystal orientation across single particles
Direct observation of dehydration pathway during solid-state transformation
evidence_signal: The abstract reports single-particle, in situ crystallographic observations showing anisotropic mass loss near water-channel surfaces followed by surface-localized nucleation and growth of anhydrous form II.
watch_item: The study is based on a model hydrate and low-dose electron measurements, so beam effects and generalization to other hydrates or processing conditions should be checked carefully.
paper_title: In Situ Dynamics of the Microscopic Crystallographic Dehydration Pathway in a Model Channel Hydrate, Theophylline
